title: 12 2 Heat Equation with insulated ends
body:
12.2 HEAT EQUATION WITH INSULATED ENDS

The equation вҲӮu вҲӮ 2u = c2 2 вҲӮt вҲӮx is called the one-dimensional heat equation. It governs the heat flow across a homogenous bar where c is determined by the thermal properties of the bar.

The adiabatic boundary conditions

ux (0, t) = 0, ux (L, t) = 0 for all time t

maintain insulated endpoints at x = 0 and x = L so that there is no heat flow across the ends of the rod. Eventually all the heat will be evenly distributed across the bar.

Initial conditions take the form:

initial temperature distribution u(x, 0) = f (x)

Solutions (eigenfunctions) are obtained via separation of variables and take the form of the constant function A0 together with nПҖx вҲ’О»2n t un (x, t) = An cos e for n = 1, 2, 3, В· В· В· L where the О»n (eigenvalues) are given by О»n = cnПҖ/L.

The general solution is the superposition of all the eigenfunctions and takes the form вҲһ X nПҖx вҲ’О»2n t u(x, t) = A0 + An cos e n=1 L

The initial temperature distribution is used to calculate the An 0 s and will require the use of Fourier series and half range expansions when the initial temperature distribution is nonsinusoidal. The steady state temperature is A0 .

Example 1 The temperature in a bar of length ПҖ satisfies the heat equation

вҲӮu вҲӮ 2u =9 2 вҲӮt вҲӮx where u(x, t) is the temperature. The bar is insulated so that the flux of heat at each end is zero. Hence:

вҲӮu вҲӮu (0, t) = (ПҖ, t) = 0 for all t. вҲӮx вҲӮx

You are also given that the initial temperature distribution is given by

пЈұ ПҖ пЈІ 0 пЈҙ 0вүӨx< ; пЈҙ u(x, 0) = f (x) = пЈҙ ПҖ пЈі 1 вүӨ x вүӨ ПҖ. пЈҙ

Express the general solution u(x, t) as a Fourier cosine series and find the steady state temperature of the system.

A sketch of the initial temperature distribution is:

You need to be careful here, as k = 0 actually yields non-trivial solutions! Let us step carefully through the method of separation of variables.

i) By assuming a solution of the form u(x, t) = F (x)G(t) show that

F 00 вҲ’ kF = 0

and G0 вҲ’ 9kG = 0 for k constant. (Note that the D.E. for G is first order!)

ii) By implementing the boundary condition

вҲӮu вҲӮu (0, t) = (ПҖ, t) = 0 for all t. вҲӮx вҲӮx show that F 0 (0) = F 0 (ПҖ) = 0.

iii) Show that k > 0 (say k = ПҒ2 ) yields the trivial solution for F .

iv) Show that k = 0 yields a constant solution for both F and G and hence that

u0 (x, t) = A0 .

v) Show that k < 0 (say k = вҲ’ПҒ2 ) yields the solution un (x, t) = An cos(nx)eвҲ’9n t .

Taking the sum of all possible solutions we have a general solution given by вҲһ X u(x, t) = A0 + An cos(nx)eвҲ’9n t n=1

We now apply the initial temperature distribution. This is tricky and will involve Fourier cosine series:

Recall that the initial temperature distribution is given by пЈұ ПҖ пЈІ 0 пЈҙ 0вүӨx< ; пЈҙ u(x, 0) = f (x) = пЈҙ ПҖ пЈі 1 вүӨ x вүӨ ПҖ. пЈҙ

Thus setting t = 0 we have вҲһ X A0 + An cos(nx) = f (x). n=1

This means that A0 and An are the Fourier cosine coefficients of f .

The even periodic extension of f has sketch:

Our final solution is therefore вҲһ   1 X вҲ’2 nПҖ вҲ’9n2 t 1 2 вҲ’9t cos(3x) вҲ’81t cos(5x) вҲ’225t u(x, t) = + sin( ) cos(nx)e = вҲ’ cos(x)e вҲ’ e + e 2 n=1 nПҖ 2 2 ПҖ 3 5

vi) Discuss the behaviour of the u(x, t) as t вҶ’ вҲһ.

LetвҖҷs take a look at an animation of the temperature flow.

http://www.math.cornell.edu/вҲјbterrell/h1/heat1.html

Try a lower resolution and:

u(x,0)=heavi(x-pi/2) Neumann Left, Neumann Right (i.e. insulated ends).

You can now do Q 118
